Facts.text: Babylonians capture Jerusalem, replace Jehoiachin with Zedekiah as king
Iran adopts the Solar Hijri calendar
York (England) Pogrom: Jews seek refuge in Clifford's Tower, which is then besieged by a mob; about 150 people are massacred or commit suicide rather than submit to Christian baptism
The Servite Order is officially approved by Cardinal Raniero Capocci, papal legate in Tuscany
The Battle of Boroughbridge takes place in the First War of Scottish Independence
Holy Spirit glides above fire in "The Miracle of Amsterdam" (legend)
Native American chief visits colony of Plymouth, Massachusetts
General Court of Election declares Rhode Island a democracy and adopts a new constitution
English Long Parliament disbands
The 23rd Regiment of Foot or Royal Welch Fusiliers is founded. In 2004 it merged with the Royal Regiment of Wales to form the Royal Welsh Regiment.
William IV Prince of Orange installed as Stadtholder of Drenthe

Louis-Antoine de Bougainville's expedition of two ships completes  first French circumnavigation of the world arriving in Saint-Malo, France (also carried 1st woman to circumnavigate the world, Jeanne Baré)

Denmark becomes the first nation to ban the transatlantic slave trade, for financial reasons. Doesn't go into effect until 1803 causing a frenzy of activity amongst slave traders who even receive financial support. An estimated 120,000 enslaved Africans were transported from Africa to the West Indies aboard vessels flying the Danish flag.
1st US Military Academy at West Point is established through Congressional act (opened July 4, 1802)
US Army Corps of Engineers established (2nd time)
Willem I proclaimed King of the Netherlands, including Belgium
First US newspaper, "Freedom's Journal" owned and operated by African Americans begins publishing in New York City
Ohio authorizes high school night classes
London's re-organised police force (Scotland Yard)
New York Stock Exchange slowest day ever (31 shares traded)
Constitution of the Republic of Texas approved, legalises slavery
Stefano Ronchetti-Monteviti's opera "Pergolese" premieres at La Scala in Milan, Kingdom of Lombardy
Arizona Territory votes to leave the Union (US Civil War)
Confederate government appoints commissioners to Britain
Battle at Pound Gap, Kentucky: Confederates separate battles
Battle of Averasboro North Carolina (1,500 casualties)
1st fertilizer law enacted
1st English FA Cup Final: Wanderers defeat Royal Engineers, 1-0, with Morton Betts scoring the winner at Kennington Oval, London
Nelly Saunders & Rose Harland fight 1st female boxing match (NY)
Susan Hayhurst becomes 1st US woman graduate of a pharmacy college
Arthur Evans rediscovers the Bronze Age city of Knossos in Crete, home of the legendary Minotaur
First 1st-class cricket game between NSW & Western Australia
Barney Oldfield uses a Benz to break the existing records at Daytona Beach Road Course (131.25mph)
Stanley Cup, Dey's Arena, Ottawa, ON: Ottawa Senators beat Port Arthur Bearcats (ON), 13-4
British battle cruisers Inflexible & Irresistible hit mines in Dardanelles
Federal Trade Commission organizes
James Barries' "Kiss for Cinderella" premieres in London
US & Canada sign migratory bird treaty
Geoffrey O'Hara's "K-K-K-Katy" song published
1 Acre Park also known as Baby Park in the Bronx renamed Melrose Park
Britain signs a trade agreement with the USSR and sends a trade mission to Moscow: this goes against the US, who in the same month refused to sign a trade agreement
Egypt achieves independence from Britain, but British troops remain
WKY-AM in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma begins radio transmissions
Hugo von Hofmannsthal's "Der Unbestechliche" premieres in Vienna
WHP-AM in Harrisburg PA begins radio transmissions
USS Constitution (Old Ironsides) floated out to become a national shrine
Genootschap Onze Taal (Our Language) organizes (Netherlands)
Adolf Hitlernames Hjalmar Schacht as President of Bank of Germany
Academy Award gold statuette is first called an Oscar in print by Sidney Skolsky
US Congress passes Migratory Bird Conservation Act
Temple defeats Colorado to win 1st NIT
Hungary annexes republic of Karpato-Ukraine
NHL record 10 goals in 1 period-NY Rangers (7), NY Americans (3) & a record 26 points in the 3rd period
German air raid on British fleet base Scapa Flow
Blizzard hits North Dakota & Minnesota killing 60
National Gallery of Art opens in Washington, D.C.
Ships Elin K (Norway) and Zaanland (Netherlands) torpedoed by German U-boats and sink
French Vichy Internal minister Pierre Pucheu sentenced to death for treason
Würzburg, Germany is 90% destroyed, with 5,000 dead, in only 20 minutes by British bombers
Convair Liner, 1st US twin-engine pressurized airplane, tested
KFMB TV channel 8 in San Diego, CA (CBS) begins broadcasting
1st annual National Book Awards
1870 mm rain in Cilaos, Island of Reunion (world record)
Josephine Kroesen appointed as 1st Dutch female judge
Toronto Maple Leafs tie NHL record 37 points beating NY Rangers 14-1
Iraq & USSR sign economic and technical treaty
Dedication of National Observatory at Kitt Peak, in the Quinlan Mountains of the Arizona-Sonoran Desert, Tohono O'odham Nation lands of Arizona dedicated
1st launching of Titan 2-rocket
US Super-Constellation disappears above Pacific Ocean, kills 167
Green Bay Packers halfbackPaul Hornungand Detroit Lions defensive tackle Alex Karras are reinstated in NFL after 1 year suspension for betting on football games
KCOY TV channel 12 in Santa Maria, California (CBS) begins broadcasting
"The Man from U.N.C.L.E." star David McCallum receives huge welcome in London
Pirate Radio Station 333 (Radio Britain) ship breaks down
General Motors produces its 100 millionth automobile, an Oldsmobile Toronado
My Lai massacre occurs when American soldiers kill ~400 unarmed Vietnamese civilians, in one of the most controversial incidents of the Vietnam War
Boston Bruins scores a NHL record 8 goals in 1 period
Viasa DC-9 crashes at Maracaibo's Grano de Oro airport, killing 155
Oxford University Press and Cambridge University press co-publish The Old Testament of the New English Bible, and Complete New English Bible, incorporating the New Testament first published in 1961
WNIN TV channel 9 in Evansville, Indiana (PBS) begins broadcasting
13th Grammy Awards: "Bridge Over Troubled Water", Carpenters win
KDCD TV channel 18 in Midland, TX (IND) suspends broadcasting
OPEC discusses raising prices to offset decline of U.S. dollar value
1st performance at new Grand Ole Opry House at Opryland in Nashville
US Mariner 10 makes 3rd & final fly-by of Mercury
Amoco Cadiz spills 223,000 tons of crude oil off French coast
Soyuz 26 returns to Earth
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
US Senate accepts Panama Canal treaty
Smallest crowd of 1,814 attend NBA Cavaliers vs. NY Nets game at Cleveland Coliseum
Gunmen kidnap William Buckley, CIA station chief in Beirut
South Africa & Mozambique sign non attack treaty
Associated Press correspondent Terry Anderson taken hostage in Beirut, Lebanon
Chemical attack on Kurdish town of Halabja by Iraqi forces kills 5000 civilians - largest ever chemical weapons attack
Federal grand jury indicts North & Poindexter in Iran-Contra affair
North Ireland Protestant fires on Catholic funeral, 3 killed
US sends 3,000 soldiers to Nicaragua's neighbor Honduras
Members of Irish Gay & Lesbian Organization march in NYC parade
NJ Net coachBill Fitchis 4th coach to win 800 NBA games
NY Lotto pays $33.3 million to one winner (#s are 18-21-32-33-35-38)
Moravcik forms Slovakia government
Manhattan upsets 4th seeded Oklahoma 77-67
Mississippi House of Representatives formally abolishes slavery & ratifies 13th Amendment of US Constitution
World best 7th wkt stand 461 by Bhupinder Singh Jr & P Dharmani
Montreal Canadien's 1st game in their new arena (Bell Center)
NJ Devils' Dave Andreychuk is 26th NHL to score 500 goals
Sandline affair: On Bougainville Island, soldiers of commander Jerry Singirok arrest Tim Spicer and his mercenaries of the Sandline International.
Largest coordinated worldwide vigil takes place as part of global protests against the Iraq War
Israel officially hands over Jericho to Palestinian control.
The United Nations General Assembly votes overwhelmingly to establish the UN Human Rights Council.
Rock Musical "Next to Normal" by Tom Kitt and Brian Yorkey premieres Off-Broadway at the Second Stage Theatre in New York (Pulitzer Prize for Drama 2010)
Parts of the Kasubi Tombs, architectural treasure and burial site of the Buganda kings, burns down in Kampala, Uganda
Turkish NATO helicopter crashes into a house killing ten people in the outskirts of Kabul, Afghanistan
24 Pakistani Army soldiers are killed in Rawalpindi after their bus falls down a ravine
A €10 billion Cyprus bailout plan will wipe out 10% of the citizens' bank deposits

Wales routs England by a record 30-3 at Millennium Stadium, Cardiff to retain their Six Nation Rugby Championship; Welsh winger Alex Cuthbert scores 2 tries and fullback Leigh Halfpenny lands 4/4 penalties
Voters in Crimea vote overwhelmingly to leave Ukraine and rejoin Russia amid international condemnation of its design
US college student Otto Warmbier is sentenced to 15 years hard labour for trying to steal a political poster, in Pyongyang, North Korea
Indian actor Irrfan Khan reveals he has been diagnosed with a neuroendocrine tumour
Beached dead whale found to have 88 pounds of plastic inside it, including 40 pounds of plastic bags, in Mabini, Philippines
COVID-19 study by London Imperial College showing migration approach could lead to 250,00 deaths in UK, over 1 million in US, prompts strategy changes from both countries
COVID-19: France orders a national lockdown, US recommends meetings 10 or less, 6 counties in San Francisco and surrounding counties told to "shelter in place"
Dow Jones Industrial Average falls 2,997 points, or 12.9%, its largest single-day point loss and its second-largest percentage drop
Global infections and deaths from COVID-19 overtake those in China for the 1st time, 86,000 vs 80,860 infections, 3,241 vs 3,208 deaths according to John Hopkins Uni
Major League Baseball suspends remainder of Spring Training and delays start of regular season in response to COVID-19 pandemic; return dependent on recommendations by Centers for Disease Control
French architects Anne Lacaton and Jean-Philippe Vassal win the Pritzker Prize for architecture
Gunman shots and kills eight people at three different spas in Atlanta, Georgia
Israeli researchers reveal re-discovery of 2,000 year-old Dead Sea Scrolls, 1st found in 60 years, left by Jews fleeing Romans in "Horror Cave", Israel
After 20 seasons as Fox's NFL lead broadcast team, Joe Buck and Troy Aikman join ESPN as new lead commentators of Monday Night Football
British-Iranians Nazanin Zaghari-Ratcliffe and Anoosheh Ashoori return to the UK after being detained for five and six years on spying charges
US Federal Reserve raises interest rates for the first time since 2018 by a quarter of a point to help ease rising price inflation
Banking jitters continues as US Wall Street banks agree to deposit US$30 billion in First Republic Bank as its stock plummets in the wake of the failure of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank
Houston Zoo announces their oldest resident Mr Pickles (90) is finally a father, with Mrs Pickles (53), the parents of three rare baby radiated tortoises, named Dill, Gherkin and Jalapeño
A fire caused by pyrotechnic devices at a nightclub hosting a concert in Kocani, North Macedonia, kills at least 59 people and injures 155 others
Asian Film Awards: Payal Kapadia’s "All We Imagine as Light" wins Best Film, Best Director Yoshida Daihachi, acting awards to Sean Lau and Shahana Goswami
Western Roman Emperor Valentinian III is assassinated in Rome by the bodyguards of GeneralFlavius Aetiusin revenge for ordering the general's death in 454
Pope Leo Xholds the last session of the Fifth Council of the Lateran, convoked to restore peace between Catholic rulers and assert the authority of Pope
Portuguese navigatorFerdinand Magellanreaches the Philippines, landing on Homonhon Island after crossing the Pacific Ocean
Battle of Khanwa: Mughal forces led byBaburdefeat a Rajpat confederacy led by Rana Sanga, aided by Mughal artillery. Afterwards Babur has a large pile built of enemy heads.
French KingLouis XIVsends troops to Ireland
Treaty of Vienna signed by PrinceEugene of Savoy, Count Sinzendorf and Count Gundacker, Thomas Stahremberg and the British envoy to Vienna, Sir Thomas Robinson.
Second Battle of Cancha Rayada - Spanish forces defeat Chileans underJosé de San Martín
Charles Darwin, aboard HMS Beagle, anchors in the recently British-acquired Falklands Islands for the first time
Nathaniel Hawthorne's "The Scarlet Letter" published by Ticknor, Reed and Fields in Boston
Edward Clark becomes Governor of Texas, replacingSam Houston, who is evicted from the office for refusing to take an oath of loyalty to the Confederacy (US Civil War)
Joseph Listerfirst outlines the discovery of antiseptic surgery in an article in "The Lancet"
Hiram R. Revelsmakes 1st official speech by an African American in the US Senate
Jules Massenet's opera "Thaïs" premieres in Paris, includes piece “Méditation” for violin and orchestra
Gustav Mahlerconducts premiere of his "Lieder eines fahrenden Gesellen" (Songs of a Wayfarer), leading the Berlin Philharmonic and Dutch baritone Anton Sistermans as soloist

AL meets in Chicago,Ban Johnsonannounces AL league will be Chicago White Stockings, Washington Senators, Milwaukee Brewers, Detroit Tigers, Cleveland Blues, Boston Americans, Philadelphia Athletics and Baltimore Orioles
Russian Grand Duke Michael, brother of TsarNicholas IIdefers acceptance of the Russian throne [OS Mar 3]
Frank Wedekind's play "Elius Erweckung" premieres in Hamburg
The free port of Fiume is formally annexed byMussolini's fascist regime.
Robert H. Goddardlaunches the first liquid-fueled rocket, goes 184' (56 meters)
Hans Lutherresigns as president of the Reichsbank and is appointed German ambassador to the United States

6th Academy Awards: "Cavalcade" and its directorFrank Lloyd,Charles Laughton(The Private Life of Henry VIII), andKatharine Hepburn(Morning Glory) win; hostWill Rogersannounces "Come and get it, Frank" andFrank Capragets up
Adolf Hitlerorders German rearmament in violation of the Treaty of Versailles
Noël Coward's musical "Operette" premieres in London
Russian composer and pianistDmitri Shostakovichreceives the Stalin Prize
Jazz musicianBillie Holidayis released early from Alderson Federal Prison Camp in West Virginia due to good behavior
AL rejects St Louis Browns ownerBill Veeck's request to move his team to Baltimore
NHL PresidentClarence Campbellsuspends Montreal Canadiens superstarMaurice "Rocket" Richardfor the remainder of the season after he viciously attacked an opponent; Riots ensued in Montreal
PresidentEisenhowerupholds the use of atomic weapons in case of war
9th Emmy Awards:Phil SilversShow, Robert Young &Loretta Youngwin
LBJasks Congress to pass Economic Opportunity Act as part of his War on Poverty
Gemini 8 launched withNeil Armstrongand David R. Scott aboard, conducts the 1st docking of two spacecraft in orbit, flight aborted after critical system failure with the crew returned safely to Earth
Robert F. Kennedyannounces presidential campaign
Northern Ireland Prime MinisterJames Chichester-Clarkmeets with British PMEdward Heathto discuss the security situation in Northern Ireland
John LennonandYoko Onoare served with deportation papers by US Immigration Department
SingerJames Brownperforms two shows for over 1,000 detainees awaiting trial at the Adolescent Remand Shelter on Rikers Island, NYC
British Prime MinisterHarold Wilsonannounces his resignation
US PresidentJimmy Carterpleads for a Palestinian homeland
Red Brigade kidnaps former premierAldo Moroin Italy, 5 killed
CBS-TV airs "Wings Over the World" withPaul McCartney
Two-time Cy Young Award winnerDenny McLainis convicted of embezzlement and drug trafficking; sentenced to 25 years but conviction later reversed

Seven members ofReba McEntire's band, and the road manager, are killed in a plane crash; one of two private jets being used by the group clipped a mountain near San Diego, California shortly after take-off, pilot and co-pilot also
Worlds Ladies' Figure Skating Champ in Munich won byKristi Yamaguchi
American figure skaterTonya Hardingpleads guilty to felony attack on former Olympic teammateNancy Kerrigan
Mike TysonTKOsFrank Brunoin 3rd round to gain heavyweight title
PopeJohn Paul IIasks God for forgiveness for the inactivity and silence of some Roman Catholics during the Holocaust
Dale Jarrettretires from points racing after the 2008 Food City 500
U.S. Postal Service issues a set of five "Latin Music Legends" stamps, including one ofCarlos Gardel
George Clooneyand other several prominent participants, including Martin Luther King III, are arrested outside the Sundanese Embassy for civil disobedience
Google commemorateAnna Atkins's 216th birthday with a Google Doodle image representing her cyanoprint work
US PresidentBarack ObamanominatesMerrick Garlandas Supreme Court Justice
US PresidentDonald Trump's second travel ban is blocked by 2 federal courts
Asia's second-richest person, Hong Kong'sLi Ka-Shing, announces his retirement at 89
Declassified US intelligence report says Russian PresidentVladimir Putinauthorized efforts to aid re-election ofDonald Trump
Ukrainian President VolodymyrZelenskyyaddresses US Congress remotely pleading for further aid against the Russian invasion
Emmanuel Macron’s government uses special powers to force through pension reform, raising the retirement age from 62 to 64, amid widespread protests
South Korea's PresidentYoon Suk-yeoland Japanese Prime MinisterFumio Kishidahold their first talks since 2011 in Tokyo, marking a milestone in relations between the two countries
KingGustav IIIof Sweden is shot by Count Jacob Johan Anckarström at a masked ball at the opera and dies on March 29
U.S. Senate ratifies the Geneva Convention of 1864, legitimatizing the International Red Cross and the American Red Cross
Thomas Beauchamp, 12th Earl of Warwick, English medieval nobleman and primary opponent of Richard II, born in England (d. 1401)
Xuande, 5th Ming Emperor of China (1425-35), born in Beijing, China (d. 1435)
Johann Geiler von Kaisersberg, prominent Swiss Protestant preacher, born in Schaffhausen, Switzerland (d. 1510)
Henry IV, Duke of Saxony from the House of Wettin, born in Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1541)
Pieter Corneliszoon Hooft, Dutch historian, poet and playwright (Grandida; Nederduytsche Historiën / History of the Netherlands), born in Amsterdam, Dutch Republic (d. 1647)[1]
Gerbrant Bredero, Dutch poet and playwright (Klucht van de Koe), born in Amsterdam, Dutch Republic (d. 1618)
Michael Franck, German composer, poet. and baker, born in Schleusingen, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1667)[1]
René Le Bossu, French literary critic (Monsieur Bossu's Treatise of the Epick Poem), born in Paris, Kingdom of France (d. 1680)[1][2]
Madame de La Fayette [Marie-Madeleine Pioche de la Vergne], French novelist (La Princesse de Clèves -1st historical novel in French), born in Paris, France (d. 1693)
Zaccaria Tevo, Italian Franciscan friar and composer, born in Piove di Sacco, Republic of Venice (d. after May 1709)
Andreas Acoluthus, German orientalist and professor of theology, born in Bernstadt, Germany (d. 1704)
Nicolas Siret, French baroque composer and organist, born in Troyes, France (d. 1754)
Sophia Dorothea of Hanover, Queen consort in Prussia and Electress of Brandenburg (1713-40), born in Hanover, Principality of Calenberg (d. 1757)
George Clymer, American merchant and Founding Father (signed US Declaration of Independence and US Constitution), born in Philadelphia, British America (d. 1813)
Johann Wilhelm Cornelius von Konigslow, German composer, born in Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1833)

Caroline Herschel, German-British pioneering female astronomer (discovered comet 35P/Herschel-Rigolle) and first elected female Honorary Members of the Royal Astronomical Society, born in Hanover, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1848)
Bengt Lidner, Swedish poet (Medea, Yttersa Domen), born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 1793)
Jean-Frédéric Waldeck, French explorer, lithographer, and cartographer, born in Prague, Czechia (d. 1875)
Juan Ramón Balcarce, Argentine military leader and politician, born in Argentina (d. 1836)
Johan Gijsbert Verstolk van Soelen, Dutch minister of Foreign Affairs, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1845)
Ami Boué, French Huguenot geologist considered to be one of the first producers of a geological map of the world, born in Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1881)
Ninkō [Ayahito], 120th Emperor of Japan (1817-46), born in Japan (d. 1846)
George Archibald McCall, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1868)
Ernst von Lasaulx, German philologist and politician, born in Koblenz, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1861)
Henry Dwight Terry, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1869)
Guildo Alary, Italian-born French composer, born in Mantua, (Napoleonic) Kingdom of Italy (d. 1891)
Ernest Feydeau, French author, friend of Flaubert (Fanny), born in Paris, France (d. 1873)
John Pope, American Major General (Union Army), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1892)
Marie-Rosalie "Rosa" Bonheur, French animalière painter (The Horse Fair; Buffalo Bill), and sculptor, born in Bordeaux, France (d. 1899)
William Henry Monk, English composer and organist (Eventide), born in London, England (d. 1889)
Charles Doolittle, American general (Union volunteer), born in Burlington, Vermont (d. 1903)
James Hector, Scottish-New Zealand geologist, naturalist, and surgeon, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1907)
Andrew Smith Hallidie, American inventor of the cable car, born in London (d. 1900)
John Butler Yeats, Irish painter, and father of W.B. Yeats, born in Lawrencetown, County Down, Ireland (d. 1922)
Sully Prudhomme, French poet and the 1st winner of the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1901, born in Paris (d. 1907)
Shibusawa Eiichi, Japanese industrialist widely known as the "father of Japanese capitalism", born in Musashi Province, Japan (d. 1931)
Magnus Gösta Mittag-Leffler, Swedish mathematician and founder of Acta Mathematica, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1927)[1]
James E. Smith, American minister and "oldest new father known to medicine" at 102 with a woman 64 years younger, born in Williamsburg, Virginia[1]
Martinus Beijerinck, Dutch microbiologist and botanist (1st to coin the name virus), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1931)
Napoléon IV, Prince Imperial, Head of the House of Bonaparte (1873-79), born in Paris, French Empire (d. 1879)
Charles Harding Firth, British historian, born in Ecclesall, Sheffield, England (d. 1936)
Bud Fowler, American Baseball HOF executive (earliest known African-American player in organized pro baseball), born in Fort Plain, New York (d. 1913)
Alexander Popov, Russian physicist and one of the first inventors of a radio receiving device, born in Turyinskiye Rudniki, Russian Empire (d. 1906)

Patsy Donovan, Irish-American baseball outfielder (Pittsburgh Pirates, St. Louis Cardinals) and manager (Pittsburgh, St. Louis, Washington Sens, Brooklyn Superbas, Boston RS), born in Queenstown, Ireland (d. 1953)
F. A. Forbes, pen name of Mother Frances Alice Monica Forbes, Scottish religious author, born in Scotland (d. 1936)
Hippolyte Daeye, Belgian expressionist painter, born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1952)
Charles Halton, British stage actor (Dr Cyclops, Tugboat Annie Sails Again), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1959)
Clemens August Graaf von Galen, German Catholic Bishop of Münster (led opposition to Nazism, especially T4 extermination program), born in Drinklage, Germany (d. 1946)
Henry B Walthall, American actor (The Birth of a Nation, Klondike), born in Shelby City, Alabama (d. 1936)
Jim Lightbody, American athlete (Olympic gold 800m, 1500m, 2590m Steeplechase 1904), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1953)
Ethel Anderson, Australian poet, essayist, novelist, and painter, born in Leamington, England (d. 1958)
Harrison Ford, American silent screen actor (Rubber Tires, Vanity Fair, Love In High Gear), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1957)
Giacomo Benvenuti, Italian composer, born in Toscolano, Italy (d. 1943)
Sydney Chaplin [Hawkes], British actor, half brother and manager of Charlie Chaplin, born in London, England (d. 1965)
Reggie Walker, South African athlete (Olympic gold 100m 1908), born in Durban, South Africa (d. 1951)
César Vallejo, Peruvian-French poet and novelist (Los Heraldos Negros; Trilce; Russia in 1931), born in Santiago de Chuco, Peru (d. 1938)
James Petrillo, American labor leader (American Federation of Musicians president, 1940-58), and radio orchestra music director (WBBM Chicago), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1984)
Isobel Elsom, British actress (My Fair Lady, Love From a Stranger), born in Cambridge, England (d. 1981)
Conrad Nagel, American actor (All That Heaven Allows, Celebrity Time), born in Keokuk, Iowa (d. 1970)
Edward Pawley, American actor (Angels with Dirty Faces), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1988)

Potti Sreeramulu, Indian revolutionary known as the Father of Andhra for conducting a 56 day hunger strike in support of a separate Telugu-speaking state, born in Nellore District, Madras, British India (d. 1952)
Lucie Rie, Austrian-born British potter, born in Vienna (d. 1995)
Mike Mansfield, American politician (Sen-D-Montana 1953-77) majority whip, born in New York City (d. 2001)
Morgan Conway, American actor (Dick Tracy, Brother Orchid), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1981)
Nikolai Lopatnikoff, Estonian-American composer (Variazioni Concertanti), born in Reval, Russian Empire (now Tallinn, Estonia) (d. 1976)
Clive Morton, British actor (Goodbye Mr Chips, Moonraker), born in London, England (d. 1975)
Elisabeth Flickenschildt, German actress, born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1977)
Francisco Ayala, Spanish writer, born in Granada, Spain (d. 2009)
Henny Youngman, American comedian and violinist ("Take my wife ... please"), born in London, England (d. 1998)
Lloyd Waner, American Baseball Hall of Fame center fielder (MLB All Star 1938; batting average over .300 x 10; Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Harrah, Oklahoma (d. 1982)
Robert Rossen, American film director and screenwriter (The Hustler, All the King's Men, Mambo), born in New York City (d. 1966)
Aladár Gerevich, Hungarian fencer (7 Olympic gold 1932, 36, 48, 52, 56, 60), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1991)
Andrew Miller-Jones, British TV pioneer (Picture Page, Dressing Up), born in London, England (d. 1994)
I. A. K. Pataudi, Indian cricket batsman (3 Tests England, 3 India; 1 x 100; Oxford University CC, Worcestershire CCC, Southern Punjab), born in Pataudi, India (d. 1952)
Norman Wooland, British character actor (Ivanhoe, Quo Vadis, Hamlet), born in Düsseldorf, Germany (d. 1989)
Dr. Josef Mengele, German SS captain and chief camp physician at Auschwitz, known as  the "Angel of Death", born in Günzburg, Germany (d. 1979)[1]
Pierre Harmel, Belgian politician and Prime Minister of Belgium (1965-66), born in Uccle, Belgium (d. 2009)
(Thelma Catherine) "Pat" Nixon [née Ryan], American First Lady (1969-74), born in Ely, Nevada (d. 1993)
Lloyd McBride, American labor leader (President of United Steelworkers), born in Farmington, Missouri (d. 1983)[1]
Mercedes McCambridge, American actress (All the King's Men, The Exorcist, Giant), born in Joliet, Illinois (d. 2004)
Samael Aun Weor [Víctor Manuel Gómez Rodríguez], Colombian author of books on esoteric spirituality and founder of the Gnostic Movement, born in Bogotá, Colombia (d. 1977)[1][2]
Aldo van Eyck, Dutch architect (City Hall), born in Driebergen, Netherlands (d. 1999)
Howard Boatwright, American composer and violinist, born in Newport News, Virginia (d. 1999)
Ernő Király, Yugoslavian composer and ethnomusicologist, born in Subotica, Kingdom of Yugoslavia (d. 2007)
Dorothea Binz, German Nazi war criminal executed for crimes at Ravensbrück concentration camp, born in Försterei Dusterlake, Germany (d. 1947)
John Addison, British Academy and BAFTA Award-winning film score composer (Tom Jones; A Bridge Too Far; Centennial), born in Surrey, England (d. 1998)
Percy Mansell, Rhodesian cricket all-rounder (13 Tests South Africa, 11 wickets, 2 x 50; Rhodesia), born in St George's, Shropshire, England (d. 1995)
Reginald "Leo" McKern, Australian actor (Blue Lagoon, Help, Mouse that Roared), born in Sydney, New South Wales (d. 2002)
Sid Fleischman, American author, and screenplay writer (The Whipping Boy; Blood Alley: The Abracadabra Kid), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2010)
Traudl Junge, German secretary to Adolf Hitler, born in Munich (d. 2002)
Anne Truitt [Anne Dean], American sculptor, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2004)
Fahd bin Abdulaziz Al Saud, King of Saudi Arabia (1982-2005), born in Riyadh, Sultanate of Nejd (d. 2005)
Geoffrey Freeman Allen, English railway writer, born in England (d. 1995)
Harding Lemay, American writer (head writer for soap "Another World"), born in North Bangor, New York (d. 2018)
Beryl Davis, British-American cabaret and big band singer (Quintette du Hot Club de France, Glenn Miller's US Army Air Force Orchestra), born in Plymouth, England (d. 2011)
Cornell Borchers, German actress (The Big Lift, Floodtide, Istanbul), born in Šilutė, Lithuania (d. 2014)
Luis E. Miramontes, Mexican chemist and co-inventor of the combined oral contraceptive pill, born in Tepic, Nayarit, Mexico (d. 2004)
Charles Goodell, American lawyer and politician (US Representative for New York (R), 1959-68; US Senator, 1968-71), born in Jamestown, New York (d. 1987)
(Reuben) "Ruby" Braff, American jazz trumpeter and cornetist, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2003)
Daniel Patrick Moynihan, American politician (US Senator from New York (D), 1977-2001), and diplomat (US ambassador to UN, 1975-79; US Ambassador to India, 1973-75), born in Tulsa, Oklahoma (d. 2003)
Karlheinz Boehm, Austrian actor (Face of Fear, Peeping Tom, Unnatural), born in Darmstadt, Germany (d. 2014)
Olga San Juan, American actress, dancer and comedian (Variety Girl, 1 Touch of Venus), born in New York City (d. 2009)
Vladimir Komarov, Soviet cosmonaut (Voshkod I, Soyuz 1) and the 1st human to die in a space flight, born in Moscow, Russia, Soviet Union (d. 1967)
